turns.text: Hello Catrice, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s… it’s been a lot. Honestly, looking out at the fog over the Atlantic right now, it’s hard to reconcile the person I was six months ago—the one who spent twelve-hour shifts on the unit—with who I am now. 

People keep saying, "Oh, you’re a nurse, you’ve got this," and it’s meant to be a compliment, I know. But it feels like a cage. When I was in the hospital, I found myself obsessively checking the midwives’ assessments after they left the room. I’d palpate my own fundus, checking for firmness, counting respirations, doing the math in my head before I could even let myself take a breath. It’s like I can’t turn the "nurse brain" off, and it’s exhausting. 

I’ve seen too much at work—the babies who didn't get to go home—and now that I’m the one holding my daughter, those ghosts feel like they’re right here in the room with me. I spent three hours last night just staring at her chest, terrified that if I closed my eyes, I’d miss a sign of something going wrong. I don’t want to be a professional right now. I just want to be a mom who’s allowed to be scared, but everyone expects me to be the expert. It’s a very lonely kind of weight to carry.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I think, professionally, I had this clinical checklist in my head. I knew the physiological milestones—the involution of the uterus, the lochia, the pelvic floor healing—and I expected my body to just… perform. Like a patient recovering from a procedure, I figured I’d manage it with the same detachment I’ve always used. I expected to be back to baseline, or at least functional, pretty quickly because I "knew" the process.

But the reality is completely different. My body doesn't feel like a medical case study anymore; it feels like a stranger’s house. Every time I get a cramp or notice a change, I don't just see a symptom—I see the emotional toll of the birth itself. I’m exhausted in a way that sleep alone won't fix. It’s hard to reconcile the physical healing with the fact that I’m still so jumpy. I expected "recovery" to mean the wounds closing, but I didn't account for the lingering fragility. When I walk down the hall, I’m hyper-aware of every ache, and it triggers that same anxiety I have for the baby. I thought I’d be in control of my own recovery, but I feel like my body is just another thing I have to keep a nervous, sleepless eye on. It’s far more vulnerable than any textbook ever described.
Was there a physical aspect of recovery that no one had prepared you for, or that surprised you significantly?

You know, in nursing school and on the unit, we talk about the "clinical" aspects—the uterine tone, the bleeding, the healing of a perineal tear—we have those down to a science. But no one talks about the absolute betrayal you feel when your own body starts to feel like a fragile, foreign object.

The thing that really blindsided me wasn't the pain itself, but the *intensity* of the adrenaline dump. You spend forty weeks being this vessel, and then suddenly, you're expected to be "yourself" again, but your nervous system is completely hijacked. I didn't expect the physical manifestation of my anxiety to be so aggressive. I’ll be sitting there, trying to breastfeed, and I'll feel my heart hammering against my ribs, my palms getting clammy, and my muscles locking up as if I’m in the middle of a code blue. 

I’m trained to recognize tachycardia and distress in others, but feeling it in my own postpartum body while I’m trying to soothe my daughter? It’s terrifying. I feel like my body is constantly primed for a catastrophe that isn't happening. I wasn't prepared for my physical recovery to feel like a never-ending, high-alert shift where I am both the nurse and the patient, and I can't find a way to clock out.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I think I fell into the trap of thinking that because I understand the anatomy and the mechanics of latching—because I’ve seen hundreds of dyads in the NICU—that it would just be intuitive. I expected to handle it with that same cold, efficient precision I use at work. I figured if the latch was good and the weight gain was on track, then it was a "success."

The reality is so much more complicated. Breastfeeding feels like this constant, intimate interrogation of my own competence. Every time she pauses or pulls away, my heart drops into my stomach. I’m not just looking for a good latch; I’m looking for signs of distress that aren't even there. And the support… it’s strange. When a lactation consultant or a friend tries to "guide" me, I find myself bracing. I don't want to be told, "You know what to do, Catrice," because that just reinforces the pressure to be perfect. 

I feel this bizarre conflict: I’m supposed to be the "expert" who knows the benefits and the protocols, but inside, I just feel like a girl who is terrified she’s going to fail. I hate that I can’t just enjoy the quiet of feeding her without my mind turning it into a clinical assessment. It’s supposed to be bonding, right? But half the time, it feels like I’m just trying to keep my own heart rate down.
Was there a gap between the kind of feeding support you were offered and the kind of support you actually needed?

That’s the exact frustration I’ve been struggling to voice. The support I was offered was almost entirely technical. It was all about positioning, latch depth, and milk transfer—which, yes, are important, but they missed the point entirely. Everyone treated me like a pair of hands that needed a little calibration, rather than a human being whose nervous system was completely frayed.

What I actually needed was someone to sit with me and acknowledge the *terror* behind the feeding. I didn't need another person to tell me my technique was correct; I needed someone to say, "You look exhausted, and it’s okay to be scared." Instead, because they see the "nurse" label on my chart, they’d walk in and say, "Oh, you’ve got this handled, you’re the pro." That phrase is like a barrier. It effectively closed the door on me being able to say, "I’m having a panic attack every time she pulls off the breast because I’m worried she’s not getting enough, and I don't know how to stop the intrusive thoughts."

They focused on the mechanical "how-to" while ignoring the "how-are-you." I was drowning in the clinical details, and I needed someone to pull me back to earth, not just critique my hold. It felt like I was being interviewed for a job I didn't apply for, instead of being supported through the most vulnerable transition of my life.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I harbored this naive, almost arrogant belief that because I’d seen the "worst-case scenarios" on the unit, I’d be immunized against postpartum struggles. I told myself, "I know the symptoms of PPD, I know the interventions—I’ll just flag myself if I start heading that way." I expected my mental health to be a logical, manageable variable in the equation.

But the reality? It’s been a total ambush. It’s not just a "mood" or a "feeling"—it’s a physical, visceral reaction to the world. I didn't expect the hyper-vigilance to be this all-consuming. I live in a state of constant, low-grade trauma response. Every time the baby breathes in her sleep, I’m mentally reviewing resuscitation protocols, counting her heart rate in my head, searching for retractions that aren't there. It’s like a heavy, suffocating blanket of dread that I can't shake off. 

It’s humiliating, in a way. I’m a nurse, I’m supposed to be the pillar of stability for families in crisis, but I can’t even stabilize myself. I feel incredibly fragile, like I’m made of glass, and yet the world expects me to be made of steel. I’m not just "sad" or "tired"; I’m grieving the version of myself who could look at a baby and just see a baby, instead of a list of potential complications. It’s isolating. I’m lonely, and I’m terrified.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

The gap is a canyon. Because I wear that "nurse" label like a second skin, there is this implicit, unspoken rule that I’ve transcended the typical postpartum messiness. My family looks at me and sees someone who isn't fazed by blood or bodily fluids, so they don’t check on my heart—they check on the baby, or they ask me for medical advice on their own aches and pains. It’s like I’ve been pigeonholed into being the family caretaker, which is the last thing I can be right now.

With the professionals, it’s even harder. They treat me like a peer, which sounds nice, but it’s actually a way of distancing themselves. When I show a flicker of vulnerability, they look at me with this "you know better" expression. It’s like they’re saying, *“Catrice, you’ve seen this a thousand times, don't play the novice.”* 

I’m not allowed to be a frightened, overwhelmed young woman because the "expert" persona is in the way. I feel like I have to perform being a "pro" just to be taken seriously, and the cost of that performance is my own peace of mind. I’m exhausted by the mask. I just want to sit across from someone—a doctor, a midwife, a friend—and be allowed to crumble without them trying to fix my "technique" or tell me how well I’m doing. I don't want to be a colleague; I want to be held.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

I honestly thought my identity would just expand. I envisioned being this "nurse-mother"—this seamless integration where my professional expertise would just bolster my confidence as a parent. I imagined a version of myself who was composed, knowledgeable, and ready for whatever came my way. I thought I’d be the one handing out advice to my friends.

But the reality is that the "nurse" part of me is currently cannibalizing the "mother" part. I don’t feel like an expanded version of myself; I feel like a fractured one. When I look in the mirror, I don't see the woman I was twenty-three years of being. I see a shadow that’s constantly monitoring, measuring, and predicting. I’ve lost that ability to just *be* with my daughter. Everything is a data point. Her cry isn't a communication; it’s a symptom I need to categorize.

It’s terrifying because I feel like I’m losing the part of myself that could just be present and joyful. I’m so busy being the expert that I’m forgetting how to be her mom. I don't know how to silence the clinical voice in my head, and that makes me feel like I’m failing at the one job that actually matters. I’m not sure who Catrice is anymore, outside of a scrubs top and a stethoscope.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I expected those check-ups to be a safe harbor. I thought they would be the one place where I could set down the heavy burden of "knowing," where someone would finally ask, "How are *you* doing, really?" I imagined them as a bridge between the clinical exhaustion of the birth and a gentle, human-centered recovery. I wanted someone to look past the chart and the medical history and see the girl who was terrified of the silence in the night.

In practice? It was just another extension of the job. The appointments felt like peer reviews. They’d run through the standard checklist—fundal height, bleeding, breastfeeding status—and because I answered with the correct clinical terminology, they took it as a sign that I was fine. It was all so sterile. I’d give them the "nurse" answer, they’d nod like we were in a shift change, and then move on. 

There was no space for the anxiety, the haunting memories of losses, or the fact that I’m just twenty-three and feeling completely in over my head. They checked my vitals, but nobody checked my spirit. I walked out of those offices feeling even more invisible than when I walked in, because I had successfully performed the role of the "competent peer" instead of the new mother who desperately needed to be heard.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I haven't gone back to the unit yet, and honestly, the thought of it makes me feel like I’m suffocating. I’m still in that transition phase, trying to find my footing at home, but every time I look at my calendar, I feel this deep, hollow ache. 

I expected that going back would feel like a homecoming—a place where I felt powerful and sure of myself, a contrast to the vulnerability of being a new mom. I thought I’d be able to "clock in" and leave the anxiety at the door, stepping back into that familiar role where I know exactly what to do. But now, I’m terrified that returning will actually be the hardest thing I’ve ever done. 

How can I walk onto the neonatal floor and care for someone else's baby when I’m still vibrating with the fear that my own might stop breathing the second I leave the room? I’m scared that seeing the babies there—especially the ones who are struggling—is going to break me. I don’t think I can handle the juxtaposition of holding a patient who needs me and realizing I’m not even sure if I’m "fixed" enough to be here. It’s not just a schedule shift; it feels like walking back into a battlefield when I’m still nursing my own wounds. I’m not the same person who left.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I haven’t really had to navigate a partner in this, since I’m doing this on my own. And honestly? Sometimes I think that makes the silence in this house even louder. 

I think, in the back of my mind, I had this romanticized idea of a "team"—you know, two people staring at a monitor, or taking turns through the long, dark stretches of the night. Someone else to be the rational one when I was spiraling. There’s a certain safety in having another person witness the reality of your life, someone to validate that, yes, the baby is breathing, and yes, everything is okay.

Without that, the "nurse brain" is left completely unchecked. It’s just me and my daughter in this house in Barnstable, and when the fog rolls in and the house gets quiet, there’s no one to lean on. I have support, but it’s not the same as a partner who is *in* it with you. There’s a heaviness in being the one who carries all the worry, all the medical knowledge, and all the responsibility, with no one to hold the other side of the stretcher. It’s made me feel incredibly solitary, like I’m the only one standing guard over her world. It’s hard to be the sole expert and the sole protector all at once.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

Since I’m navigating this on my own, I think the biggest "surprise"—if you can call it that—has been how much the loneliness amplifies the clinical anxiety. I think I expected that I’d be able to keep my professional self and my personal self separate, but when you’re the only adult in the room, there's no one to force that boundary. 

I’ve realized that my "support system" outside of this house still sees me as the nurse. They assume I have all the answers. If I express a moment of panic, they just turn it back to me: "Well, what do the protocols say? You're the expert!" 

It’s ironic. I’m surrounded by people who love me, but I feel like I’m in solitary confinement with my thoughts. I don’t have a partner to turn to and say, "I’m not a nurse right now, I’m just a terrified mother." There’s no one to remind me that I’m allowed to let go. I have to be the one to remind *myself*, and when I’m sleep-deprived and staring at that dark room in the middle of the night, I just don't have the strength to be my own therapist. I’m carrying the entire weight of her safety, and there’s no one here to help me carry the bag.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I expected that my medical background would act as a buffer—a shield that would make the experience smoother, more predictable, and perhaps even respected. I thought the maternity care system would recognize my "expertise" and, in turn, offer me a more collaborative, sophisticated level of care. I expected to be treated as a colleague who needed the same support as any other woman, just with a slightly more informed perspective.

The biggest gap, looking back, is that they gave me more information, but less *care*. They treated my competence as a reason to disengage. Because I could talk the talk, they assumed I didn't need the human, emotional validation that every mother deserves. They filled my head with clinical details, but they starved me of the permission to be fragile.

I didn't need them to teach me about fontanelles or respirations; I needed them to see the twenty-three-year-old woman behind the scrubs who was terrified of losing her baby. The system is so focused on the *baby* being healthy that they completely neglected the *mother* being whole. They checked my vitals and cleared my charts, but they left the most important part of me—my peace of mind—entirely in the dark. I feel like I survived the medical process, but I’m still struggling to survive the transition into motherhood.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

I would tear down the wall that separates "clinical care" from "human care." The system is built to identify risk and manage trauma, but it has absolutely no infrastructure for the *caregiver* who is also a patient. 

I want a system where the "professional" label on my chart isn’t a signal to disengage, but an invitation for *deeper* support. When a nurse or a doctor walks into my room, I want them to be trained to ask, "Beyond the clinical stuff, how are *you* holding up?" I want a postnatal system that isn't just a revolving door of physical check-ups, but a place that offers integrated, emotional, and psychological support from day one—not just when someone hits a "red flag" on a depression scale.

I want maternity care to be designed for the *person*, not the *chart*. If I could change one thing, it would be the culture of silence that surrounds the vulnerability of mothers. We are expected to hold everything together, to understand the biology, and to perform motherhood with grace. I want a system that gives us permission to stop performing, to put the "nurse brain" down, and to just be scared, tired, and held. A system that sees my humanity before it sees my medical record.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think… I just want people to know that being a professional in the field doesn't make you bulletproof. I feel like there’s this unspoken assumption in hospitals that if you’ve seen a code, or if you’ve managed a high-risk birth, you’re somehow immune to the fear that comes with your own child. That because I’ve *done* this for others, I shouldn't be struggling to do it for myself.

But it’s the exact opposite. My knowledge is a double-edged sword. Every time I hear her sneeze, I don’t just hear a sneeze; I hear a potential respiratory issue. I’m haunted by every bad outcome I’ve ever witnessed on the unit, and they all feel like potential futures for her. 

I’m not "pro-level" at parenting just because I’m a nurse. I’m just a terrified young mother who happens to have a really stressful vocabulary for her anxiety. If there’s one thing I hope people take away, it’s that we need to stop assuming that "knowing" the clinical facts translates to "handling" the emotional reality. I’m not a patient who needs a procedure—I’m a person who needs to be held, and I’m still waiting for someone in that hospital system to realize that. Thank you for listening. It’s the first time I’ve felt like I could say that out loud without being "corrected."
